title: MVCC
question: How does PostgreSQL's MVCC work?
answer: Multiversion Concurrency Control keeps multiple row versions so readers never block writers and vice versa. Old versions are cleaned up by VACUUM.
explanation: MVCC enables snapshot isolation without heavy locking.
codeExample: VACUUM (VERBOSE, ANALYZE) mytable;
bestPractices: Monitor autovacuum lag.
commonMistakes: Long transactions preventing vacuuming.
interviewTips: Contrast with SQL Server RCSI.

title: JSONB
question: What is jsonb and when should you use it?
answer: jsonb stores JSON as a decomposed binary format supporting indexes (GIN), containment operators, and fast field access. Use it for semi-structured data.
explanation: Prefer jsonb over json unless preserving whitespace/order.
bestPractices: Model relational data as tables; use jsonb for flexible attrs.
commonMistakes: Storing everything as jsonb losing relational strengths.
interviewTips: Explain @> and jsonb_path_query.

title: CTEs
question: What are CTEs and how do you use them?
answer: Common Table Expressions (WITH ...) create temporary named result sets that can reference each other and can be recursive.
explanation: Recursive CTEs walk hierarchies (org charts, trees).
bestPractices: Use CTEs for readability; be aware of Postgres 12+ CTE inlining.
commonMistakes: Using CTEs as unnecessary optimization fences.
interviewTips: Show a recursive CTE example.

title: Extensions
question: Name a few useful Postgres extensions.
answer: pg_stat_statements (query stats), pgcrypto (crypto), postgis (spatial), uuid-ossp (UUIDs), pg_trgm (fuzzy search), TimescaleDB (time series).
explanation: Install with CREATE EXTENSION.
codeExample:
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;
SELECT * FROM t WHERE name % 'searhc'; -- fuzzy match
bestPractices: Keep an inventory of installed extensions.
commonMistakes: Missing extensions on replicas.
interviewTips: Discuss when to use extensions vs external services.

title: Index Types
question: What index types does PostgreSQL offer and when do you use each?
answer: B-tree (default, equality & range on scalar/ordered data), Hash (equality only), GIN (multi-value columns like arrays, jsonb, full-text), GiST (geometric, ranges, nearest-neighbor), SP-GiST (partitioned trees), and BRIN (huge, naturally ordered tables with tiny index footprint).
explanation: Choosing the right access method matters more than adding many indexes. B-tree covers most OLTP lookups; GIN powers containment (@>) and full-text; GiST handles overlap/KNN; BRIN is ideal for append-only time-series where physical order correlates with a column, giving huge space savings.
bestPractices: Match the access method to the query pattern and data shape.
Use partial and covering (INCLUDE) indexes to shrink size.
Prefer BRIN for append-only time-series to save space.
commonMistakes: Adding a hash index where a B-tree is needed for ranges.
Over-indexing write-heavy tables and slowing INSERT/UPDATE.
interviewTips: Explain why GIN is used for jsonb and arrays.
Mention BRIN's trade-off: tiny size vs coarse granularity.

title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE
question: How do you read EXPLAIN ANALYZE output to diagnose a slow query?
answer: EXPLAIN shows the planner's estimated plan; EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs it and reports real timings, row counts, and loops. Compare estimated vs actual rows to spot bad statistics, and look for expensive Seq Scans, nested loops with high loop counts, or spills to disk.
explanation: Large gaps between estimated and actual rows usually mean stale statistics (run ANALYZE) or correlated predicates the planner can't estimate. Watch for Sort/Hash nodes that report 'Disk' usage (raise work_mem), and Seq Scans on big tables where an index would help. BUFFERS reveals cache vs disk reads.
codeExample:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT o.id, c.name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.created_at >= now() - interval '7 days';
-- Look for: 'Seq Scan', actual rows vs estimated, 'Buffers: read=' spikes
bestPractices: Always use ANALYZE and BUFFERS for real diagnosis.
Run ANALYZE to refresh statistics before blaming the planner.
Reproduce with realistic data volumes, not tiny dev tables.
commonMistakes: Reading plain EXPLAIN estimates as if they were real timings.
Ignoring the estimated-vs-actual row mismatch.
interviewTips: Describe how you'd fix a Seq Scan showing on a huge table.
Explain what 'rows removed by filter' indicates.

title: VACUUM & Autovacuum
question: Why is VACUUM necessary and how does autovacuum work?
answer: Because of MVCC, updates and deletes leave dead tuples that must be reclaimed. VACUUM marks that space reusable, updates the visibility map, and (with ANALYZE) refreshes statistics. Autovacuum runs automatically based on how many rows have changed relative to configurable thresholds.
explanation: Regular VACUUM prevents table bloat and transaction ID (XID) wraparound. Autovacuum triggers when dead tuples exceed autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + scale_factor * reltuples. Long-running transactions hold back the xmin horizon and prevent cleanup, causing bloat. VACUUM FULL rewrites the table and reclaims disk to the OS but takes an exclusive lock.
bestPractices: Let autovacuum run; tune scale factors for hot tables.
Monitor dead tuple counts via pg_stat_user_tables.
Avoid long idle-in-transaction sessions that block cleanup.
commonMistakes: Disabling autovacuum to 'save resources'.
Running VACUUM FULL routinely and locking tables.
interviewTips: Explain XID wraparound and why it's dangerous.
Distinguish VACUUM from VACUUM FULL.

title: Transaction Isolation
question: What transaction isolation levels does PostgreSQL support?
answer: Read Committed (default), Repeatable Read, and Serializable. Postgres does not have dirty reads even at the lowest level. Repeatable Read uses a snapshot for the whole transaction; Serializable adds Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) to prevent anomalies.
explanation: Read Committed takes a fresh snapshot per statement, so non-repeatable reads are possible. Repeatable Read prevents non-repeatable and phantom reads via a transaction-level snapshot but can raise serialization failures on write conflicts. Serializable detects dangerous read/write dependency cycles and aborts one transaction with a serialization_failure that the app should retry.
bestPractices: Wrap Serializable transactions in retry-on-40001 logic.
Keep transactions short to reduce conflict windows.
Pick the lowest isolation level that is still correct.
commonMistakes: Assuming Repeatable Read serializes writes across rows.
Not handling serialization failures in application code.
interviewTips: Explain SSI vs traditional two-phase locking.
Note that Postgres never allows dirty reads.

title: Window Functions
question: What are window functions and how do they differ from GROUP BY?
answer: Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them into one output row. Unlike GROUP BY, each input row is preserved, and you can rank, run totals, or compare to neighbors using OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...).
explanation: The OVER clause defines the window: PARTITION BY splits rows into groups, ORDER BY sets ordering for ranking and frames. Functions include ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG/LEAD, and aggregates like SUM used as windows for running totals. Frames (ROWS/RANGE BETWEEN) control which rows are included.
codeExample:
SELECT
  employee_id,
  department,
  salary,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank,
  SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_total
FROM employees;
bestPractices: Use window functions instead of self-joins for running totals.
Name windows with WINDOW clause when reused.
commonMistakes: Confusing RANK (gaps) with DENSE_RANK (no gaps).
Expecting WHERE to filter on a window result (use a subquery).
interviewTips: Explain the difference between ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK.
Show a running total with SUM() OVER (ORDER BY ...).

title: Arrays
question: How do you work with array columns in PostgreSQL?
answer: Postgres supports true array types (e.g. int[], text[]). You can store, index (GIN), and query them with operators like = ANY(...), @> (contains), && (overlap), and functions like unnest(), array_agg(), and array_length().
explanation: Arrays are useful for tags or small ordered lists where a separate table would be overkill. unnest() expands an array into rows for joining; array_agg() collapses rows back into an array. A GIN index accelerates containment and overlap searches. For large or heavily-relational data, prefer a normalized child table.
bestPractices: GIN-index arrays used with @> or && filters.
Use unnest()/array_agg() to bridge relational and array forms.
commonMistakes: Using arrays where a normalized join table is more maintainable.
Forgetting arrays are 1-based indexed in Postgres.
interviewTips: Contrast array columns vs a many-to-many join table.
Explain @> vs && operators.

title: Full-Text Search
question: How does PostgreSQL full-text search work?
answer: Full-text search converts documents into tsvector (normalized lexemes) and queries into tsquery, matching with the @@ operator. A GIN index on the tsvector makes searches fast, and ranking functions like ts_rank order results by relevance.
explanation: to_tsvector applies a language configuration to tokenize, stem, and drop stop words; to_tsquery/plainto_tsquery/websearch_to_tsquery build the search expression. Store a generated tsvector column and GIN-index it for performance. For fuzzy/typo matching, combine with pg_trgm; for heavy search needs, a dedicated engine may be better.
bestPractices: Store a generated tsvector column and GIN-index it.
Pick the correct text search configuration (language).
commonMistakes: Calling to_tsvector on every query row without an index.
Using LIKE '%term%' when full-text search is appropriate.
interviewTips: Explain tsvector vs tsquery and the @@ operator.
Mention pg_trgm for fuzzy matching.

title: Table Partitioning
question: What is declarative partitioning and when should you use it?
answer: Partitioning splits one large logical table into smaller physical partitions by RANGE, LIST, or HASH of a key. It improves manageability and enables partition pruning so queries scan only relevant partitions, and lets you drop old data cheaply by dropping a partition.
explanation: Declarative partitioning (Postgres 10+) uses PARTITION BY on the parent and CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF for children. Range partitioning by date is common for time-series: pruning skips irrelevant partitions, and DROP/DETACH removes old data instantly without a heavy DELETE. Index each partition; the planner prunes at plan or execution time.
bestPractices: Partition on a column present in most WHERE clauses.
Automate partition creation/retention with a scheduler.
Keep partition counts reasonable to avoid planning overhead.
commonMistakes: Partitioning small tables that don't need it.
Querying without the partition key, defeating pruning.
interviewTips: Explain partition pruning and retention via DROP TABLE.
Compare RANGE vs LIST vs HASH partitioning.

title: Materialized Views
question: How do materialized views differ from regular views?
answer: A regular view is a stored query executed every time it's referenced. A materialized view stores the query's result on disk, so reads are fast, but the data is a snapshot that must be refreshed with REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW.
explanation: Use materialized views to cache expensive aggregations or joins that don't need to be perfectly current. REFRESH ... CONCURRENTLY avoids locking readers but requires a unique index and is slower. You can index a materialized view like a table. The trade-off is staleness vs query speed.
bestPractices: Add a unique index to enable REFRESH CONCURRENTLY.
Schedule refreshes based on acceptable staleness.
commonMistakes: Expecting a materialized view to auto-update on base changes.
Refreshing non-concurrently and locking out readers.
interviewTips: Explain the staleness vs speed trade-off.
Note the unique-index requirement for CONCURRENTLY.

title: Sequences, SERIAL & IDENTITY
question: What's the difference between SERIAL and GENERATED ... AS IDENTITY?
answer: Both auto-generate numbers from a sequence. SERIAL is legacy sugar that creates a sequence and sets a default. GENERATED ... AS IDENTITY is the SQL-standard approach that ties the sequence to the column, offering cleaner ownership and GENERATED ALWAYS to block manual inserts.
explanation: IDENTITY columns are recommended over SERIAL: the sequence is fully owned by the column, permissions are simpler, and GENERATED ALWAYS prevents accidental manual values (override with OVERRIDING SYSTEM VALUE). Sequences are non-transactional for gaps—nextval() consumed by a rolled-back transaction is not reused, so gaps are normal and expected.
bestPractices: Prefer GENERATED ... AS IDENTITY over SERIAL for new tables.
Accept sequence gaps; don't rely on contiguous IDs.
commonMistakes: Assuming sequences never leave gaps.
Using SERIAL and losing track of sequence ownership.
interviewTips: Explain why IDENTITY is preferred over SERIAL.
Note that sequences are exempt from transaction rollback.

title: Upsert with ON CONFLICT
question: How do you perform an upsert in PostgreSQL?
answer: Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (conflict_target) DO UPDATE or DO NOTHING. When a unique or primary key conflict occurs, Postgres either updates the existing row (using the special EXCLUDED pseudo-table) or silently skips the insert.
explanation: The conflict target must match a unique constraint or index. EXCLUDED refers to the row proposed for insertion, letting you merge values. DO NOTHING is handy for idempotent inserts. This is atomic and avoids the race conditions of a manual SELECT-then-INSERT pattern.
codeExample:
INSERT INTO inventory (sku, qty)
VALUES ('A-100', 5)
ON CONFLICT (sku)
DO UPDATE SET qty = inventory.qty + EXCLUDED.qty
RETURNING sku, qty;
bestPractices: Ensure a matching unique constraint exists for the conflict target.
Use RETURNING to get the resulting row in one round trip.
commonMistakes: Specifying a conflict target with no unique index.
Referencing the table name instead of EXCLUDED for new values.
interviewTips: Explain the EXCLUDED pseudo-table.
Contrast ON CONFLICT with a race-prone SELECT-then-INSERT.

title: LATERAL Joins
question: What is a LATERAL join and when is it useful?
answer: A LATERAL join lets a subquery in the FROM clause reference columns from preceding tables in the same FROM. It's ideal for top-N-per-group queries and for calling set-returning functions per row.
explanation: Without LATERAL, a subquery in FROM can't see the outer row. With LATERAL, it runs once per outer row, enabling patterns like 'the 3 most recent orders per customer'. It pairs naturally with LIMIT inside the subquery and with functions like jsonb_array_elements() or generate_series().
codeExample:
SELECT c.id, c.name, o.id AS order_id, o.created_at
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT id, created_at
  FROM orders
  WHERE orders.customer_id = c.id
  ORDER BY created_at DESC
  LIMIT 3
) o ON true;
bestPractices: Use LATERAL for top-N-per-group instead of window+filter when clearer.
Combine with LEFT JOIN LATERAL ... ON true to keep unmatched rows.
commonMistakes: Forgetting ON true with LEFT JOIN LATERAL.
Using a correlated subquery in SELECT when LATERAL is cleaner.
interviewTips: Explain why the subquery can reference the outer table.
Show a top-N-per-group example.

title: CTEs vs Subqueries
question: When should you use a CTE versus a subquery?
answer: Both factor out logic, but CTEs (WITH) are named, can be referenced multiple times, and support recursion. Subqueries are inline. Since Postgres 12, non-recursive CTEs are inlined by the planner unless marked MATERIALIZED, so performance differences are usually small.
explanation: Use CTEs for readability, recursion, or when a result is reused several times. Before Postgres 12, CTEs were an optimization fence (always materialized), which could hurt performance; now the planner inlines them. Add MATERIALIZED to force one-time evaluation, or NOT MATERIALIZED to force inlining. Subqueries remain fine for simple, single-use expressions.
bestPractices: Prefer CTEs for readability and reuse; subqueries for simple one-offs.
Use MATERIALIZED/NOT MATERIALIZED to control planner behavior when needed.
commonMistakes: Assuming CTEs are always an optimization fence (fixed in PG12).
Repeating an expensive subquery instead of naming it once.
interviewTips: Mention the PG12 CTE inlining change.
Explain MATERIALIZED vs NOT MATERIALIZED hints.

title: Constraints & Exclusion
question: What constraint types does PostgreSQL support, including exclusion constraints?
answer: Standard constraints are NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, and CHECK. PostgreSQL adds EXCLUSION constraints, which generalize uniqueness: they guarantee that for any two rows, a set of operators (e.g. overlap &&) does not all return true—perfect for preventing overlapping time ranges.
explanation: Exclusion constraints use an index (typically GiST) and operators to reject conflicting rows. A classic use is preventing double-booking: no two reservations for the same room may have overlapping time ranges. CHECK constraints validate row-level conditions; FOREIGN KEYs enforce referential integrity with configurable ON DELETE/UPDATE actions.
bestPractices: Enforce invariants in the database with constraints, not just app code.
Use exclusion constraints to prevent overlapping ranges.
commonMistakes: Relying only on application logic for uniqueness/overlap rules.
Forgetting btree_gist for equality columns in exclusion constraints.
interviewTips: Describe a real use case for EXCLUDE (no double-booking).
Explain CHECK vs FOREIGN KEY vs UNIQUE.

title: Generated Columns & TOAST
question: What are generated columns and how does TOAST store large values?
answer: A generated column is computed from other columns via GENERATED ALWAYS AS (expr) STORED, kept consistent automatically. TOAST (The Oversized-Attribute Storage Technique) transparently compresses and/or moves large field values (beyond ~2KB) into a side table so main table rows stay compact.
explanation: Stored generated columns persist the computed value and can be indexed; Postgres does not yet support VIRTUAL generated columns in stable releases. TOAST kicks in for large variable-length types (text, jsonb, bytea): values are compressed and, if still large, stored out-of-line, retrieved only when accessed. This keeps sequential scans fast when big columns aren't selected.
codeExample:
CREATE TABLE people (
  first_name text,
  last_name text,
  full_name text GENERATED ALWAYS AS (first_name || ' ' || last_name) STORED
);

-- Control TOAST compression strategy for a big column
ALTER TABLE docs ALTER COLUMN data SET STORAGE EXTERNAL; -- store out-of-line, no compression
bestPractices: Use STORED generated columns for derived values you filter or index on.
Let TOAST handle large columns; avoid selecting them when not needed.
commonMistakes: Expecting VIRTUAL generated columns (not yet in stable Postgres).
Selecting large TOASTed columns unnecessarily and slowing scans.
interviewTips: Explain the ~2KB TOAST threshold and out-of-line storage.
Note generated columns are recomputed on write, not read.

title: Roles, WAL & Replication
question: How do roles/permissions, WAL, and replication fit together in PostgreSQL?
answer: Roles are unified users/groups controlled with GRANT/REVOKE. The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) records every change before it hits data files, guaranteeing durability and crash recovery. Streaming replication ships WAL to standby servers, which replay it to stay in sync—synchronously or asynchronously.
explanation: Every committed change is written to WAL first (fsync), so after a crash Postgres replays WAL to recover. Standbys consume that WAL stream for high availability and read scaling. Synchronous replication waits for a standby to confirm before commit (stronger durability, higher latency); asynchronous is faster but can lose the last transactions on failover. Roles with LOGIN act as users; NOINHERIT/INHERIT and membership control privilege flow.
bestPractices: Grant least privilege via group roles, not per-user grants.
Monitor replication lag with pg_stat_replication.
Choose sync vs async replication based on durability needs.
commonMistakes: Giving application roles superuser privileges.
Assuming async replicas never lose recent transactions on failover.
interviewTips: Explain why WAL guarantees durability (write-ahead principle).
Contrast synchronous vs asynchronous replication trade-offs.
